I INTRODUCTION 
 
I.1 Product name  
OMI_EXTREME_WAVE_BLKSEA_wave_power 
I.2 Indicator evaluation method 
The Wave Power P is defined by: 
𝑃 = 𝜌𝑔2
64𝜋 𝐻𝑠
2𝑇𝑒 
Where ρ is the surface water density, g the acceleration due to gravity, Hs the 
significant wave height (VHM0), and Te the wave energy period (VTM10)  also 
abbreviated with Tm-10. The extreme statistics and related recent changes are 
defined by (1) the 99th percentile of the Wave Power, (2) the linear trend of 
99th percentile of the Wave Power, and (3) the difference (anomaly) of the 
99th percentile of the last available year in the multiyear dataset compared 
against the long-term average. The statistics are based on the period 1950 to -
1Y and are obtained from yearly averages.

II SCIENTIFIC VALIDATION 
II.1 Black Sea extreme wave events 
More details and full scientific evaluation can be found in the CMEMS Ocean 
State Report issue 6 (von Schuckmann et al., 2022), Sect. 2.8.  
As the variables of this OMI cannot be validated directly, selected validations 
of the significant wave height (SWH) are shown. The SWH and wave energy 
period of the product BLKSEA_MULTIYEAR _ WAV_007_006 are the basis of the 
wave power computations and a thorough validation of th ese parameters is 
supposed to provide reliable results also for the wave power parameters. 
The most exte nsive validation of SWH was done by comparing L3 altimetry 
satellite data with the model data interpolated to the satellite data  (Figure 1). 
It is important to mention that the data of these satellite , namely Sentinel -3a/-
3b, Cryosat -2, CFOSat, Saral/Altik a, and Envisat,  has not been assimilation as 
done for the Jason missions. Reassuringly, the model skill is nearly identical to 
the values obtained for the assimilated satellite data. Root mean square 
differ
